label: Basic Vocabulary
description: Essential words and phrases introduced in the overview of the Telugu language.

label: Fill in the Blank
description: Practice sentence structure, suffixes, and formality levels.

items.prompt: In Telugu, the sentence 'I play cricket' is structured as 'Nēnu cricket '.
items.explanation: Telugu follows a Subject-Object-Verb (S-O-V) order, so the verb 'play' (āḍatānu) comes at the end.

items.prompt: To say 'Please do' politely, you add the suffix '-aṇḍi' to 'chēyi' to get 'Chēy'.
items.explanation: The suffix '-aṇḍi' is used to make a command or request polite or formal.

items.prompt: When speaking to an elder like Murthy, you should address him as 'Murthy_______'.
items.explanation: '-gāru' is a common honorific suffix added to names to show respect, similar to 'Sir' or 'Ji'.

items.prompt: If you are speaking to a close friend of the same age, the correct word for 'You' is '_______'.
items.explanation: 'Nuvvu' is the informal version of 'you' used for friends or younger people.

label: Grammar & Tips
description: Key structural and phonetic features of Telugu explained.

items.title: Sentence Structure (S-O-V)
items.body: Unlike English (Subject-Verb-Object), Telugu uses Subject-Object-Verb. You announce the characters first, then the object they are interacting with, and finally the action.

items.title: Agglutination (Stacking Suffixes)
items.body: Telugu is an agglutinative language. Meanings are modified by 'stacking' suffixes onto a root word in a predictable, algorithmic way. This can create long words that represent entire phrases.

items.title: Retroflex Sounds
items.body: Telugu features retroflex consonants (ṭ, ḍ, ṇ, ļ). To pronounce these, curl the tip of your tongue back to touch the roof of your mouth. In transliteration, these are marked with a dot underneath.

example.english: Similar to 't' in the American pronunciation of 'city'.

items.title: Formality Levels
items.body: Telugu has strict formality levels for 'You'. 'Mīru' is formal/respectful (elders, strangers), while 'Nuvvu' is informal/casual (friends, younger people). Using the wrong one can be seen as impolite.
